source_file: zhihu_F_1留学生从OPT到H_1B_中间有哪些需要注意的关键节点.txt

heuristics.rule: Apply for OPT 60-90 days before graduation; USCIS processing takes 3-5 months
heuristics.condition: When you are an F-1 student approaching graduation and planning to use OPT work authorization
heuristics.action: Submit the OPT application to your DSO and then USCIS 60-90 days before your program end date to ensure EAD arrives before you need to start working
heuristics.because: USCIS processing time for EAD is 3-5 months; late applications risk a gap between graduation and work authorization

heuristics.rule: Notify DSO and update SEVIS within 10 days of changing employers on OPT
heuristics.condition: When an OPT holder changes jobs or employers during the OPT period
heuristics.action: Report the employer change to your DSO within 10 days so SEVIS records are updated
heuristics.because: OPT requires active SEVIS maintenance; failure to update in time can be treated as a violation of status

heuristics.rule: Start H-1B sponsorship conversations with your employer from OPT year one, not the final year of STEM OPT
heuristics.condition: When you are an F-1 student on OPT or STEM OPT who needs H-1B sponsorship
heuristics.action: Raise H-1B sponsorship intentions with your employer during your first OPT year to allow time for internal approval processes
heuristics.because: Most employers require time to navigate internal legal and budget approvals; waiting until the last year of STEM OPT leaves insufficient runway

heuristics.rule: Confirm employer will pay for premium processing before H-1B is filed
heuristics.condition: When your employer is filing an H-1B petition on your behalf
heuristics.action: Confirm upfront that the employer will cover premium processing ($2,805) to receive a decision within 15 business days
heuristics.because: Without premium processing, the approval timeline creates a status limbo period where you cannot be certain of your authorization continuity

heuristics.rule: Write a genuine, role-specific I-983 Training Plan — do not use a generic template
heuristics.condition: When applying for STEM OPT extension and submitting the I-983 Training Plan
heuristics.action: Customize the I-983 to specifically describe how the job tasks connect to your STEM degree field
heuristics.because: Multiple community members confirmed that copy-paste template I-983 forms triggered NOIDs (Notices of Intent to Deny)

decision_factors.why_it_matters: The 90-day unemployment cap is cumulative across the entire OPT period; once exhausted, status is violated regardless of future employment
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Track every gap between jobs carefully; subtract all previous unemployment periods from the 90-day total to know your remaining buffer

decision_factors.factor: Whether your degree appears on the official STEM OPT eligible list
decision_factors.why_it_matters: STEM OPT extension is only available for designated STEM programs; an unlisted degree disqualifies you regardless of job function
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check the official DHS STEM Designated Degree Program List before assuming eligibility

decision_factors.why_it_matters: STEM OPT requires the employer to be enrolled in E-Verify; a non-enrolled employer blocks the extension even if all other conditions are met
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask the employer HR or legal team directly whether they are registered on E-Verify before accepting an offer if STEM OPT is your plan

decision_factors.factor: H-1B lottery probability across multiple attempts
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Community-cited probability is approximately 50-60% per attempt over three tries; planning only for year one success without contingencies is risky
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Calculate how many lottery attempts your current OPT and STEM OPT timeline allows, and identify fallback paths before the first attempt

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Universities and qualified nonprofit research institutions are exempt from the H-1B cap, offering a path to H-1B status without lottery exposure
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Research whether target employers or adjacent institutions qualify as cap-exempt; evaluate whether the role type fits

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Changing jobs during OPT without tracking cumulative unemployment days
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: A 60-day gap between job one and job two consumes most of the 90-day total, leaving only 30 days of buffer for any future gap — a fact many people discover only after the second transition
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: OPT holders in volatile industries or who job-hop early in their OPT period

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Waiting until the final year of STEM OPT to raise H-1B sponsorship with employer
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Internal employer approval processes can take months; by the time approval is granted, the H-1B registration window may have already passed
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: STEM OPT holders at large corporations or companies with bureaucratic HR and legal processes

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Using a generic template for the I-983 Training Plan
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: USCIS may issue a Notice of Intent to Deny (NOID), requiring a burdensome response and potentially jeopardizing STEM OPT approval
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who underestimate the I-983 as a formality and rely on shared or downloaded templates

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Not paying for H-1B premium processing
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Standard processing leaves a prolonged uncertainty window during which the applicant cannot confirm their work authorization status for the upcoming fiscal year
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Applicants whose employers are unwilling to absorb the premium processing fee and who have no savings buffer for job disruption

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating the 90-day OPT unemployment limit as a per-episode cap rather than a cumulative total
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The word 'cap' implies a reset or per-incident limit; the cumulative nature is counterintuitive and not prominently communicated by universities
failure_patterns.consequence: Students exhaust their buffer earlier than expected, triggering an out-of-status situation without realizing it
warning_signs: Student has already had one employment gap of 45+ days
Student is between jobs and not actively tracking cumulative days

failure_patterns.pattern: Submitting a copy-paste generic I-983 Training Plan
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students perceive the I-983 as a bureaucratic checkbox rather than a substantive document; templates are widely shared in communities
failure_patterns.consequence: USCIS issues a NOID, requiring significant time and legal resources to respond, and potentially denying the STEM OPT extension
warning_signs: Student downloaded an I-983 template from a forum or peer
Training plan descriptions are vague or not tied to specific job duties

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming H-1B sponsorship is implicitly agreed to without an explicit early conversation
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate an employer being 'visa-friendly' with a firm commitment to sponsor; the topic feels awkward to raise early
failure_patterns.consequence: By the time the student raises H-1B in year two or three, internal processes, budget freezes, or policy changes prevent the employer from committing in time for the registration window
warning_signs: Student has never explicitly discussed H-1B with HR or manager
Student is in final year of STEM OPT without a confirmed sponsor

diagnostic_questions.question: How many cumulative days have you been unemployed since your OPT start date?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person is approaching or has exceeded the 90-day cumulative unemployment cap
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If over 60 days: How quickly is your next job starting? Do you have documentation of active job search?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If under 30 days: You have meaningful buffer remaining — but track every future gap carefully

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your degree program on the official DHS STEM Designated Degree Program List?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person is actually eligible for STEM OPT or is assuming eligibility based on the field name alone
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Proceed to confirm employer E-Verify status
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: STEM OPT extension is not available; plan H-1B strategy around standard 12-month OPT only

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your employer registered on E-Verify?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether STEM OPT is actually accessible at this employer, regardless of other eligibility factors
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Proceed with I-983 preparation and STEM OPT application
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: STEM OPT cannot be used at this employer; evaluate whether employer will register or whether a different employer is needed

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you had an explicit, documented conversation with your employer about H-1B sponsorship?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person has a real commitment or is operating on an assumption that could fail at the worst moment
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Has the employer confirmed they will cover premium processing costs?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Raise H-1B sponsorship immediately; do not wait — internal approvals take time

industry_realities.common_belief: STEM OPT is tied to the employer, so changing jobs means restarting the STEM OPT application
industry_realities.actual_reality: STEM OPT authorization is tied to the individual, not the employer. When changing jobs, the new employer simply needs to submit a new I-983 Training Plan; the authorization period itself does not restart
industry_realities.implication: Students should not avoid job changes out of fear of losing STEM OPT; they just need to ensure the new employer is E-Verify enrolled and file the new I-983 promptly

industry_realities.common_belief: H-1B lottery and STEM OPT are mutually exclusive or interfere with each other
industry_realities.actual_reality: H-1B registration and STEM OPT run concurrently without conflict. You can participate in the H-1B lottery while on STEM OPT, and if selected, transition to H-1B status on October 1st
industry_realities.implication: Students should register for H-1B every eligible year regardless of STEM OPT status; missing a lottery year wastes an attempt

industry_realities.common_belief: The H-1B master's cap gives a significant advantage, effectively guaranteeing selection
industry_realities.actual_reality: The 20,000 master's cap slots are drawn first, but unselected master's-cap applicants are then entered into the regular 65,000 pool — so master's degree holders get two chances per registration, not a guaranteed outcome
industry_realities.implication: Master's holders should still plan for lottery failure; the advantage is meaningful but not determinative given overall selection rates

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Planning horizon for H-1B sponsorship conversations
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Raises H-1B topic with employer only when STEM OPT is nearing expiration or after lottery registration opens
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Initiates H-1B sponsorship conversation during OPT year one, well before any deadline, treating it as a career planning discussion rather than a visa emergency
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Employer internal approval processes — legal review, budget allocation, policy vetting — can take months; early conversations create runway that last-minute conversations cannot

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes each job gap is evaluated independently and believes 90 days resets with each new employer
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Maintains a running log of every unemployment day from OPT start date, treats the 90-day total as a finite, non-renewable resource
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Students who misunderstand the cumulative nature burn through their buffer unknowingly and trigger out-of-status situations

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Contingency planning if H-1B is not selected
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Focuses entirely on winning the lottery; has no defined fallback plan
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Simultaneously evaluates cap-exempt employers, alternative visa categories (O-1, TN), further education for a new OPT cycle, and Canada as a backup jurisdiction before the first lottery attempt
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: With per-attempt odds of roughly 50-60% across three tries, students without contingencies face a crisis rather than a transition when they go unselected

community_misconceptions.misconception: The 90-day OPT unemployment limit resets each time you start a new job
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The phrase '90-day limit' sounds like a per-episode allowance; people intuitively expect a fresh start with each new employer
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: The 90-day unemployment limit is a cumulative total across the entire OPT period; all gaps between jobs add together and count against a single shared total
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: A student who had a 60-day gap early in OPT, then faces a second gap, may believe they still have 90 days available — in reality they have only 30, and may go out of status without knowing it

community_misconceptions.misconception: STEM OPT is tied to the employer, so switching jobs requires reapplying for STEM OPT
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The I-983 is employer-specific and must be resubmitted; students conflate resubmitting the form with restarting the entire authorization
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: STEM OPT authorization belongs to the individual student; changing employers requires submitting a new I-983 with the new employer but does not reset or restart the authorization period
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Students may stay in jobs they want to leave — or pass on better opportunities — out of unfounded fear that switching will cost them their STEM OPT status

community_misconceptions.misconception: The I-983 Training Plan is a formality that can be filled out using a standard template
community_misconceptions.why_believed: It is framed as an administrative document; shared templates are widely available in student communities and feel like sufficient compliance
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: USCIS evaluates the I-983 for substantive specificity; a generic or templated plan has resulted in NOIDs (Notices of Intent to Deny) for real applicants
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Receiving a NOID requires legal response, delays authorization, and risks denial of the STEM OPT extension entirely

real_case_outcomes.situation: Student submitted an I-983 Training Plan using a widely circulated generic template rather than writing job-specific content
real_case_outcomes.decision: Used copy-paste template language describing training in vague, non-role-specific terms
real_case_outcomes.outcome: Received a NOID from USCIS requiring a substantive written response, causing delays and legal costs
real_case_outcomes.lesson: The I-983 is evaluated for genuine specificity; using templates creates a documentable pattern USCIS recognizes and flags — every student's I-983 must reflect their actual job duties tied to their specific degree

psychological_barriers.trigger: Student asks whether they should raise H-1B sponsorship with their employer 'too early' and worries about seeming presumptuous or creating awkwardness
psychological_barriers.underlying_state: Fear of rejection or of signaling desperation; avoidance of a high-stakes conversation that could reveal bad news
psychological_barriers.how_it_manifests: Student delays the conversation year after year, reframing inaction as strategic patience, until the window for meaningful employer preparation has closed
psychological_barriers.expert_move: Reframe the conversation as career planning, not visa begging — experienced advisors encourage framing H-1B as part of a long-term retention discussion, which most employers appreciate having early

psychological_barriers.trigger: Student asks 'what if I don't get H-1B three years in a row' and focuses the question entirely on lottery probability statistics
psychological_barriers.underlying_state: Catastrophizing combined with decision paralysis; the student is seeking reassurance rather than actually planning contingencies
psychological_barriers.how_it_manifests: Student continues to operate as if H-1B is the only viable path, ignoring cap-exempt employers, O-1 eligibility, TN visa (if applicable), Canadian pathways, or re-enrollment options
psychological_barriers.expert_move: Shift from probability discussion to contingency mapping — force the student to name a specific Plan B before discussing Plan A odds, which dissolves the paralysis by making alternatives concrete

reframing_moves.original_framing: What are my chances of getting H-1B and what should I do if I don't get it?
reframing_moves.reframe: You have up to three lottery attempts during STEM OPT — treat each as one of several parallel tracks, not a single binary outcome. The question is not 'will I get H-1B' but 'what is my full portfolio of paths and which ones am I actively building?'
reframing_moves.why_it_works: Shifts the mental model from passive lottery participant to active multi-path strategist; reduces anxiety by distributing it across actionable options rather than concentrating it on a single uncertain event
reframing_moves.example_language: If 3年内没中H-1B: 继续读书、Cap-exempt雇主、O-1、TN、转赴加拿大 — treat these as a real list, not a footnote

reframing_moves.original_framing: I need to wait and see if I get H-1B before talking to my employer about sponsorship
reframing_moves.reframe: The H-1B registration window is fixed, but your employer's internal approval process is not — it can take months. The conversation you delay today is the preparation your employer cannot do in time
reframing_moves.why_it_works: Exposes the hidden asymmetry: the student thinks they are waiting for the right moment, but the employer clock is already running; the reframe makes inaction visibly costly
reframing_moves.example_language: 从OPT第一年就要和雇主谈H-1B意愿，不要等到STEM OPT最后一年才开始

_extraction_key: zhihu_cleaned_zhihu_F_1留学生从OPT到H_1B_中间有哪些需要注意的关键节点
